era_context: The early 13th century was characterized by the rapid expansion of the Mongol Empire under Ögedei Khan, the fragmentation of Islamic dynasties, the consolidation of European feudal kingdoms, and the final decades of the Crusader states. The Jin Dynasty in northern China was under increasing pressure from Mongol forces, and the Southern Song Dynasty was preparing for the inevitable Mongol conquest.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Oceania and Pacific islands (no written records)
Americas (no written records in Old World sources)

events.title: Mongol-Jin Dynasty conflict continues; Jin Dynasty in decline
events.description: The conflict between the Mongol Empire and the Jin Dynasty continued in 1230. The Jin Dynasty, already weakened by decades of conflict with the Mongols, was facing increasing pressure. The Mongols, under Ögedei Khan, were consolidating their control over northern China and preparing for the final assault on the Jin state. This conflict would culminate in the siege of Kaifeng in 1232–1234 and the complete destruction of the Jin Dynasty.

sources.name: Yuanshi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)
Jin Dynasty records and Chinese chronicles

events.certainty_note: The Mongol-Jin conflict in 1230 is documented in Chinese and Mongol sources, though specific events in 1230 are less clearly dated than later campaigns.

events.title: Reconquista advances in Iberia; Christian kingdoms expand
events.description: The Reconquista, the Christian reconquest of Iberia from Muslim rule, continued to advance in 1230. Christian kingdoms, particularly Castile and Aragon, were expanding southward at the expense of the declining Almohad Dynasty. The year 1230 saw continued Christian military successes and territorial gains. This period marks the acceleration of the Reconquista, which would culminate in the fall of Granada in 1492.

sources.name: Chronicles of the Reconquista, such as those by Lucas of Tuy

events.certainty_note: The Reconquista's advance in 1230 is documented in both Christian and Islamic sources, though specific battles and dates are sometimes uncertain.

events.title: Iltutmish consolidates power in the Delhi Sultanate
events.description: Shams al-Din Iltutmish, the founder of the Slave Dynasty in Delhi, was consolidating his power in 1230. He had established Turkish rule in northern India and was expanding the sultanate's territory. Iltutmish was establishing the administrative and military structures that would define the Delhi Sultanate for centuries. His reign (1211–1236) was a period of expansion and consolidation.
key_figures: Shams al-Din Iltutmish (Sultan of Delhi, r. 1211–1236)
events.certainty_note: Iltutmish's reign is documented in Islamic chronicles, though specific events in 1230 are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Almohad Dynasty in decline; fragmentation of North Africa
events.description: The Almohad Dynasty was in visible decline in 1230. The dynasty was losing territory in Iberia to Christian kingdoms and facing challenges from rising dynasties in North Africa, particularly the Hafsids in Tunisia and the Marinids in Morocco. The Almohads' centralized authority was fragmenting, and their territorial holdings were shrinking. This period marks the transition toward the late medieval political fragmentation of the Maghreb.
events.certainty_note: The decline of the Almohads is documented in Islamic chronicles, though the precise chronology is sometimes debated.
cross_references: 1212-001 (Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa, earlier Almohad setback)

events.title: Mongol consolidation of control over Central Asia
events.description: Mongol forces continued to consolidate control over Central Asia in 1230, establishing administrative structures and eliminating remaining pockets of resistance. These campaigns were part of Ögedei Khan's broader strategy of expanding Mongol dominion. The consolidation of Mongol control facilitated trade and communication across the Silk Road.
key_figures: Chagatai Khan (Mongol ruler of Central Asia)

sources.name: Jami' al-tawarikh (Compendium of Chronicles) by Rashid al-Din

events.certainty_note: Mongol consolidation in Central Asia is documented in Islamic and Mongol sources, though specific events in 1230 are less clearly dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact chronology of the Mongol-Jin conflict in 1230 is uncertain; some sources conflate events from 1230 with those from 1231–1234. The significance of 1230 as a turning point is debated. The Reconquista's advance in 1230 is documented, but the specific battles and their significance are sometimes disputed. The decline of the Almohads is a gradual process, and 1230 is not universally recognized as a significant turning point.
historiographic_note: The year 1230 is moderately well-documented for East Asia (Mongol-Jin conflict), the Islamic world (Almohad decline, Mongol consolidation), and Europe (Reconquista advances). The historical record is weighted toward political and military events. Economic and social developments are underrepresented. Chinese, Islamic, and European sources dominate; African, Southeast Asian, and American sources are absent.

graph_edges.note: The Mongol-Jin conflict in 1230 led to intensified Mongol campaigns in 1231 and the siege of Kaifeng in 1232.
The Reconquista advances in 1230 were part of the broader Christian reconquest of Iberia that culminated in the fall of Granada in 1492.
The decline of the Almohads in 1230 led to Al-Ma'mun's accession in 1232.
